projects.title: Object Literals Basics
projects.description: Create an object literal with properties and a method. Access properties using dot and bracket notation.
projects.exampleText: const person = { name: 'Alice', age: 30, greet() { return `Hi, I'm ${this.name}`; } };
projects.exampleOutput: Name: Alice; Age: 30; Greeting: Hi, I'm Alice; Also using bracket: Alice
projects.learningOutcome: Understanding object literals, property access, and methods.
projects.logicExplanation: Object literals are the simplest way to create objects. They consist of key-value pairs, where keys are strings (or Symbols) and values can be any data type, including functions (methods).
projects.codeExplanation: This code creates an object `person` with properties (name, age) and a method `greet`. It demonstrates accessing properties with dot and bracket notation, and calling the method.

projects.title: Dot vs Bracket Notation
projects.description: Demonstrate accessing properties using dot notation and bracket notation, including dynamic keys.
projects.exampleText: const user = { name: 'John', 'favorite color': 'blue' };
projects.exampleOutput: Dot: John; Bracket (static): John; Bracket (dynamic with variable): John; Bracket for property with space: blue
projects.learningOutcome: Differences between dot and bracket notation.
projects.logicExplanation: Dot notation is cleaner but only works with valid identifier names. Bracket notation allows any string, including those with spaces or dynamic keys (using variables).
projects.codeExplanation: The script shows accessing the same property using both notations, and demonstrates bracket notation with a variable key.

projects.title: Adding, Updating, Deleting Properties
projects.description: Add new properties, update existing ones, delete a property, and check for property existence using `in` and `hasOwnProperty()`.
projects.exampleText: let obj = {};
projects.exampleOutput: After adding: { name: 'John' }; After update: { name: 'Jane' }; After delete: {}; hasOwnProperty check: false
projects.logicExplanation: Objects are mutable. You can add new properties by assignment, update existing ones, and delete properties with the `delete` operator. The `in` operator checks for a property in the object or its prototype chain, while `hasOwnProperty` checks only the object's own properties.
projects.codeExplanation: The script starts with an empty object, then adds properties, updates one, deletes another, and shows how to check for existence using `in` and `hasOwnProperty`.

projects.title: Nested Objects and Arrays
projects.description: Create an object with nested objects and arrays. Access deeply nested values and modify them.
projects.exampleText: const company = { name: 'TechCorp', address: { city: 'SF', zip: '94105' }, employees: [{ name: 'Alice' }, { name: 'Bob' }] };
projects.exampleOutput: City: SF; First employee: Alice; Modify nested value: zip changed to '94107'.
projects.logicExplanation: Objects can contain other objects or arrays as property values. Accessing deeply nested values requires chaining dot or bracket notation. Safe navigation can be done with optional chaining (covered later).
projects.codeExplanation: The code defines a `user` object with a nested `address` object and an array of `hobbies`. It demonstrates accessing properties at multiple levels, modifying nested values, and iterating over the array.

projects.title: Accessing Deeply Nested Values
projects.description: Access deeply nested values using traditional chaining and optional chaining to avoid errors.
projects.exampleText: const data = { user: { profile: { name: 'Alice' } } };
projects.exampleOutput: Traditional: Alice; Optional chaining: Alice (if exists), otherwise undefined.
projects.logicExplanation: Accessing deeply nested values can be done by chaining dot or bracket notation. However, if any intermediate property is missing, an error occurs. Optional chaining (`?.`) provides a safer way.
projects.codeExplanation: This script shows both the traditional way and the safe way using optional chaining to access a deeply nested property that may not exist.

projects.title: The this Keyword Inside Objects
projects.description: Demonstrate how `this` works inside object methods, and compare regular functions vs arrow functions regarding `this` binding.
projects.exampleText: const obj = { name: 'Test', regular() { console.log(this.name); }, arrow: () => { console.log(this.name); } };
projects.logicExplanation: Inside object methods, `this` refers to the object that called the method. Arrow functions do not have their own `this`; they inherit from the enclosing scope. Understanding this distinction is crucial for object behavior.
projects.codeExplanation: The script defines an object with a regular method and an arrow function method. It logs the output of each, showing how `this` behaves differently. It also shows a standalone function to demonstrate global `this`.

projects.title: Arrow vs Normal Functions: this
projects.description: Show how arrow functions capture the surrounding `this` while normal functions have their own.
projects.exampleText: const counter = { count: 0, normal: function() { this.count++; }, arrow: () => { this.count++; } };
projects.exampleOutput: normal works; arrow fails because `this` is not the counter.
projects.logicExplanation: General object manipulation project covering Arrow vs Normal Functions: this.
projects.codeExplanation: The JavaScript code implements Arrow vs Normal Functions: this as described in the example.

projects.title: Iterating Objects: for...in
projects.description: Use `for...in` to iterate over object properties, and filter own properties with `hasOwnProperty`.
projects.exampleText: const obj = { a: 1, b: 2 };
projects.logicExplanation: `for...in` iterates over enumerable properties (including inherited). To iterate only own properties, combine with `hasOwnProperty`.
projects.codeExplanation: The script shows `for...in` with and without `hasOwnProperty` to filter inherited properties.

projects.title: Object.keys, Object.values, Object.entries
projects.description: Extract keys, values, and key-value pairs from an object using the static methods.
projects.exampleText: const car = { brand: 'Toyota', model: 'Camry', year: 2020 };
projects.learningOutcome: Using Object static methods to convert objects to arrays.
projects.logicExplanation: General object manipulation project covering Object.keys, Object.values, Object.entries.
projects.codeExplanation: The JavaScript code implements Object.keys, Object.values, Object.entries as described in the example.

projects.title: Looping Arrays of Objects with map, filter, reduce
projects.description: Apply higher-order functions on an array of objects: filter adults, map to names, reduce to sum ages.
projects.exampleText: [{name:'Alice',age:25},{name:'Bob',age:17},{name:'Charlie',age:20}]
projects.learningOutcome: Combining array methods on object arrays.
projects.logicExplanation: When working with arrays of objects, `map()`, `filter()`, and `reduce()` are powerful. `map` transforms each object into something else, `filter` selects a subset, and `reduce` aggregates values.
projects.codeExplanation: The script creates an array of user objects. It filters users older than 18, maps to their names, and reduces to sum ages. All operations are shown in sequence.

projects.title: Objects as Reference Types
projects.description: Show that objects are reference types: assign one object to another, modify, and both change.
projects.exampleText: let obj1 = { a: 1 }; let obj2 = obj1; obj2.a = 2;
projects.exampleOutput: obj1.a = 2, obj2.a = 2; obj1 === obj2 is true
projects.logicExplanation: Objects are reference types; variables store a reference to the object, not the object itself. Assigning an object to another variable copies the reference.
projects.codeExplanation: The code shows reference behaviour by modifying an object through a second variable, and demonstrates that strict equality (`===`) compares references, not content.

projects.title: Shallow Copy: Spread and Object.assign
projects.description: Create a shallow copy using spread and Object.assign. Show that nested objects are shared.
projects.exampleText: const original = { a: 1, b: { c: 2 } };
projects.logicExplanation: A shallow copy duplicates top-level properties, but nested objects are still shared. Shallow copies can be created with the spread operator (`...`) or `Object.assign()`.
projects.codeExplanation: The script creates a shallow copy using spread and `Object.assign`, and shows that modifying a nested property in the copy affects the original.

projects.title: Deep Copy: JSON and structuredClone
projects.description: Create a deep copy using JSON methods and structuredClone. Show that changes don't affect the original.
projects.exampleText: const original = { a: 1, b: { c: 2 } };
projects.exampleOutput: Copy's nested change does NOT affect original.
projects.logicExplanation: A deep copy creates a fully independent copy, including all nested objects. Two common methods: `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj))` (works for JSON-serializable data) and `structuredClone()` (modern, handles more types).
projects.codeExplanation: The code creates a deep copy using both methods, then modifies nested properties in the copies to show they don't affect the original.

projects.title: Shorthand Properties and Computed Names
projects.description: Use shorthand property syntax and computed property names when creating objects.
projects.exampleText: const name = 'Alice', age = 30; const key = 'city';
projects.logicExplanation: Object literal shorthand allows you to write `{ name, age }` when variable names match property names. Computed property names let you use expressions inside brackets, e.g., `{ [key]: value }`.
projects.codeExplanation: The code uses shorthand to create an object from variables, and demonstrates a computed property name with a dynamic key.

projects.title: Object Destructuring
projects.description: Extract properties using destructuring, including nested objects and default values.
projects.exampleText: const user = { name: 'Alice', address: { city: 'NYC' }, age: undefined };
projects.logicExplanation: Destructuring extracts properties from an object into variables. Nested destructuring digs into nested objects. Default values can be provided for missing properties.
projects.codeExplanation: The script destructures a user object, including nested address properties, and shows default values for optional fields.

projects.title: Spread and Rest Operator for Objects
projects.description: Use spread to merge objects, and rest to collect remaining properties during destructuring.
projects.exampleText: const obj1 = { a: 1, b: 2 }; const obj2 = { c: 3 };
projects.exampleOutput: Merged: { a:1, b:2, c:3 }; Rest: { a:1 } from { a:1, b:2, c:3 }
projects.logicExplanation: General object manipulation project covering Spread and Rest Operator for Objects.
projects.codeExplanation: The JavaScript code implements Spread and Rest Operator for Objects as described in the example.

projects.title: Default Values in Destructuring
projects.description: Provide default values when destructuring to handle missing properties.
projects.exampleText: const { name = 'Anonymous', age = 18 } = {};
projects.logicExplanation: General object manipulation project covering Default Values in Destructuring.
projects.codeExplanation: The JavaScript code implements Default Values in Destructuring as described in the example.

projects.title: Getters and Setters
projects.description: Define a getter and setter for an object property to add logic.
projects.exampleText: person = { firstName: 'John', lastName: 'Doe', get fullName() { ... }, set age(value) { ... } };
projects.exampleOutput: fullName: 'John Doe'; set age(30) triggers validation.
projects.logicExplanation: Getters and setters allow you to define computed properties or add validation when reading/writing a property. They are defined using `get` and `set` keywords.
projects.codeExplanation: The script defines a `person` object with a getter for full name (combining first and last) and a setter for age with validation.

projects.title: Optional Chaining and Nullish Coalescing
projects.description: Use `?.` to safely access nested properties and `??` to provide fallback values.
projects.exampleText: const user = { profile: { name: 'Alice' } };
projects.learningOutcome: Safe property access and default values.
projects.logicExplanation: Optional chaining (`?.`) safely accesses nested properties without throwing an error if a reference is null or undefined. Nullish coalescing (`??`) provides a default value only when the left side is `null` or `undefined`.
projects.codeExplanation: The code demonstrates safe access to a deeply nested property that may not exist, using `?.`, and uses `??` to supply a fallback.

projects.title: Object.freeze, Object.seal, Object.preventExtensions
projects.description: Demonstrate the effects of freezing, sealing, and preventing extensions on an object.
projects.exampleText: const obj = { a: 1 };
projects.exampleOutput: Frozen: cannot modify; Sealed: can modify but not add/delete; PreventExtensions: cannot add.
projects.logicExplanation: General object manipulation project covering Object.freeze, Object.seal, Object.preventExtensions.
projects.codeExplanation: The JavaScript code implements Object.freeze, Object.seal, Object.preventExtensions as described in the example.

projects.title: Constructor Functions
projects.description: Create a constructor function to generate multiple objects.
projects.exampleText: function Person(name, age) { this.name = name; this.age = age; }
projects.logicExplanation: Constructor functions are a classic way to create multiple similar objects. They are called with `new`, which creates a new object, sets its prototype, and binds `this` to the new object.
projects.codeExplanation: The code defines a `Person` constructor with `name` and `age`. It creates two instances and shows their properties.

projects.title: Prototype and Prototype Chain
projects.description: Show how prototype chain works by creating an object that inherits from another using `Object.create()`.
projects.exampleText: const parent = { greet() { return 'Hello'; } }; const child = Object.create(parent);
projects.logicExplanation: Every JavaScript object has a prototype (an internal link to another object). When you access a property, the engine walks up the prototype chain until it finds it or reaches `null`. This enables inheritance.
projects.codeExplanation: The code creates a parent object with a method, then creates a child using `Object.create(parent)`. It demonstrates property lookup through the prototype chain and checks the prototype with `isPrototypeOf`.

projects.title: Adding Methods Using Prototypes
projects.description: Add a method to a constructor's prototype so all instances share it.
projects.exampleText: function Person(name) { this.name = name; } Person.prototype.sayHi = function() { return `Hi ${this.name}`; };
projects.logicExplanation: Methods can be added to a constructor's prototype to be shared across instances, saving memory.
projects.codeExplanation: The script extends a `Person` constructor by adding a `greet` method to the prototype, then creates instances that can use it.

projects.title: ES6 Class Syntax
projects.description: Define a class with a constructor, instance method, and static method.
projects.exampleText: class Circle { constructor(radius) { this.radius = radius; } area() { ... } static describe() { ... } }
projects.logicExplanation: ES6 classes provide a cleaner syntax for constructors and inheritance. A class has a `constructor` method, instance methods (defined without `function`), and static methods (called on the class itself).
projects.codeExplanation: The script defines a `Rectangle` class with `constructor`, `area` instance method, and a static `describe` method. It creates an instance and calls both types of methods.

projects.title: Class Inheritance using extends
projects.description: Create a subclass that extends a parent class using `extends`.
projects.exampleText: class Square extends Rectangle { constructor(side) { super(side, side); } }
projects.logicExplanation: Classes can inherit from another class using `extends`. The `super()` call in the child constructor runs the parent constructor. Methods can be overridden by defining them again in the child class.
projects.codeExplanation: The script defines a `Rectangle` class and a `Square` class that extends it. It uses `super` to call the parent constructor, overrides the `area` method, and adds a new method.

projects.title: super Keyword and Method Overriding
projects.description: Use `super()` in the child constructor and override a parent method.
projects.exampleText: class Square extends Rectangle { constructor(side) { super(side, side); } area() { return super.area(); } }
projects.exampleOutput: Square overrides area but calls parent.
projects.logicExplanation: `super` is used in child classes to call the parent constructor or parent methods. It is required in the child constructor before using `this`.
projects.codeExplanation: The code shows a `Vehicle` class and a `Car` class that extends it. The `Car` constructor calls `super` with brand and model, and the `start` method uses `super.start()` to call the parent method.

projects.title: JSON.parse and JSON.stringify
projects.description: Convert an object to a JSON string and back.
projects.exampleText: const obj = { name: 'Alice', age: 30 };
projects.exampleOutput: JSON string: '{"name":"Alice","age":30}'; parsed object equal to original.
projects.logicExplanation: `JSON.stringify()` converts an object to a JSON string, and `JSON.parse()` converts back. This is used for data exchange and storage.
projects.codeExplanation: The code stringifies an object, logs the JSON, then parses it back into a new object, showing they are equal but not the same reference.

projects.title: Storing Objects in LocalStorage
projects.description: Save an object to localStorage and retrieve it after page reload.
projects.exampleText: const user = { name: 'Alice', age: 30 };
projects.exampleOutput: After refresh, retrieved object matches original.
projects.logicExplanation: General object manipulation project covering Storing Objects in LocalStorage.
projects.codeExplanation: The JavaScript code implements Storing Objects in LocalStorage as described in the example.

projects.title: Merging Objects
projects.description: Merge two objects using spread operator or Object.assign.
projects.logicExplanation: Objects can be merged using spread (`{ ...obj1, ...obj2 }`) or `Object.assign()`. When merging, later properties overwrite earlier ones.
projects.codeExplanation: The script merges two objects with overlapping keys and shows the result.

projects.title: Checking Object Equality
projects.description: Compare objects by reference and by content (shallow) using JSON.stringify.
projects.exampleText: objA = { a: 1, b: 2 }; objB = { a: 1, b: 2 };
projects.logicExplanation: Comparing objects with `===` checks reference equality. To compare content (shallow), you can stringify or implement a deep comparison function.
projects.codeExplanation: The code shows reference equality vs. content equality using JSON stringification, and notes that this works only for JSON-serializable data.

projects.title: Combining Object Methods: Filter, Map, Reduce on Objects
projects.description: Use Object.entries and array methods to transform an object (e.g., filter out properties with falsy values, map values).
projects.exampleText: const obj = { a: 1, b: null, c: 3, d: 0 };
projects.logicExplanation: General object manipulation project covering Combining Object Methods: Filter, Map, Reduce on Objects.
projects.codeExplanation: The JavaScript code implements Combining Object Methods: Filter, Map, Reduce on Objects as described in the example.

projects.title: Property Descriptors: Enumerable, Writable, Configurable
projects.description: Explore property descriptors using `Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptor` and `Object.defineProperty`.
projects.exampleText: Object.defineProperty(obj, 'hidden', { enumerable: false, value: 42 });
projects.exampleOutput: for...in does not show 'hidden', but obj.hidden exists.
projects.logicExplanation: General object manipulation project covering Property Descriptors: Enumerable, Writable, Configurable.
projects.codeExplanation: The JavaScript code implements Property Descriptors: Enumerable, Writable, Configurable as described in the example.

projects.title: Object.create and Custom Prototypes
projects.description: Use Object.create to set a custom prototype for an object.
projects.exampleText: const proto = { greet() { return 'Hi'; } }; const obj = Object.create(proto);
projects.logicExplanation: General object manipulation project covering Object.create and Custom Prototypes.
projects.codeExplanation: The JavaScript code implements Object.create and Custom Prototypes as described in the example.

projects.title: Mixins: Copying Methods from Multiple Sources
projects.description: Implement a mixin pattern by copying methods from multiple objects into a target using Object.assign.
projects.exampleText: const flyer = { fly() { return 'Flying'; } }; const swimmer = { swim() { return 'Swimming'; } }; const duck = {};
projects.logicExplanation: General object manipulation project covering Mixins: Copying Methods from Multiple Sources.
projects.codeExplanation: The JavaScript code implements Mixins: Copying Methods from Multiple Sources as described in the example.

projects.title: Object.keys with Sorting and Grouping
projects.description: Extract keys, sort them, and group values by some condition.
projects.exampleText: const scores = { Alice: 85, Bob: 92, Charlie: 78 };
projects.logicExplanation: General object manipulation project covering Object.keys with Sorting and Grouping.
projects.codeExplanation: The JavaScript code implements Object.keys with Sorting and Grouping as described in the example.

projects.title: The delete Operator and Property Removal
projects.description: Use `delete` to remove a property and understand its effects.
projects.exampleText: const obj = { a: 1, b: 2 }; delete obj.a;
projects.logicExplanation: General object manipulation project covering The delete Operator and Property Removal.
projects.codeExplanation: The JavaScript code implements The delete Operator and Property Removal as described in the example.

projects.title: Checking if Object is Empty
projects.description: Write a function to check if an object has no own enumerable properties.
projects.exampleText: isEmpty({}) => true; isEmpty({ a: 1 }) => false
projects.logicExplanation: General object manipulation project covering Checking if Object is Empty.
projects.codeExplanation: The JavaScript code implements Checking if Object is Empty as described in the example.

projects.title: Nested Destructuring with Defaults
projects.description: Combine nested destructuring and default values for complex objects.
projects.exampleText: const data = { user: { name: 'Alice' } }; const { user: { name, age = 18 } } = data;
projects.logicExplanation: General object manipulation project covering Nested Destructuring with Defaults.
projects.codeExplanation: The JavaScript code implements Nested Destructuring with Defaults as described in the example.

projects.title: Object.fromEntries: Converting Arrays to Objects
projects.description: Use `Object.fromEntries` to convert an array of key-value pairs back to an object.
projects.exampleText: const entries = [['a',1], ['b',2]]; const obj = Object.fromEntries(entries);
projects.logicExplanation: General object manipulation project covering Object.fromEntries: Converting Arrays to Objects.
projects.codeExplanation: The JavaScript code implements Object.fromEntries: Converting Arrays to Objects as described in the example.

projects.title: Comprehensive Object Practice
projects.description: Combine multiple object concepts: create a library of books (array of objects), add/remove books, search by title, save to localStorage, etc.
projects.exampleText: Library app with add, remove, search.
projects.logicExplanation: General object manipulation project covering Comprehensive Object Practice.
projects.codeExplanation: The JavaScript code implements Comprehensive Object Practice as described in the example.
